hypothesis: A naive top-k dense-retrieval RAG pipeline over our vendor-agreement corpus can flag clause violations in an uploaded contract with acceptable factual reliability.
method: naive RAG: top-5 dense retrieval, single-shot generation
conclusion: Hallucinated clause citations 22% of the time — model invents clause numbers not present in retrieved context. Unusable for legal review. Retrieval recall also weak with zero overlap.

hypothesis: Increasing chunk size and adding overlap will raise retrieval recall enough to reduce downstream hallucination.
method: naive RAG with larger overlapping chunks
conclusion: Recall improved to 0.83 but hallucination only dropped to 18%. Confirms the failure is in generation/faithfulness, not retrieval — better context alone does not stop fabricated citations.

hypothesis: A RAGAS-based offline eval harness (faithfulness + answer relevance + context precision) will give us a reliable, cheap signal to iterate against without manual review every run.
method: RAGAS faithfulness / answer-relevance / context-precision on a fixed 60-query eval set
conclusion: Eval harness works and correlates with manual review (Spearman 0.74). Faithfulness is the metric that tracks the hallucination problem. Now we can iterate cheaply. This is the eval foundation for all later hallucination work.

hypothesis: Adding a Chain-of-Verification post-processing pass (draft -> generate verification questions -> answer against context -> revise) will cut hallucinated clause citations substantially at modest extra cost.
method: Chain-of-Verification (CoVe) over top-8 retrieval; verify each cited clause exists in retrieved context before finalizing
conclusion: Best result so far: hallucinated clause rate 22% -> 7%, faithfulness 0.62 -> 0.78. Cost/latency up ~30% but acceptable. Remaining 7% are clause-number near-misses. Next: targeted decoding constraints or a grounded citation verifier.

hypothesis: A cross-encoder reranker on top-25 candidates will tighten context precision and squeeze out the remaining near-miss clause hallucinations.
method: retrieve top-25 dense, rerank to top-8 with cross-encoder, then CoVe
conclusion: Reranking improved context precision (0.66 -> 0.81) but faithfulness/hallucination barely moved (7% -> 6%). Diminishing returns — the bottleneck is generation grounding, not retrieval ranking. Recommend focusing remaining budget on generation-side grounding, not more retrieval tuning.

hypothesis: A ReAct tool-using agent can autonomously resolve customer support tickets (refunds, package verification, inventory updates) end-to-end with acceptable accuracy.
method: ReAct loop with tools: issue_refund, check_shipment, update_inventory, lookup_order
conclusion: Agent resolves 74% of tickets but issues wrongful refunds 12% of the time — it calls issue_refund before confirming the package was actually lost or the order exists. Financially unacceptable. Core problem: refund action taken on unvalidated premises (hallucinated order state).

hypothesis: Gating the issue_refund tool behind a mandatory validation step (must call check_shipment + lookup_order and get positive confirmation first) will sharply cut wrongful refunds.
method: ReAct with a precondition guard: refund tool rejects calls unless validation tools returned confirming results in the same trace
conclusion: Wrongful refunds 12% -> 4% with only a 3pt drop in resolution rate. Hard precondition gating works better than prompt-only instructions. Remaining 4% are cases where validation tools themselves returned ambiguous results the agent over-trusted.

hypothesis: Adding a Reflexion-style self-critique pass before any irreversible action (refund) will catch the remaining over-trusted-validation errors.
method: Reflexion: before issue_refund, agent writes a verbal critique of its own evidence and must pass its own check
conclusion: Self-critique pass brought wrongful refunds 4% -> 2.5% on hard cases. Cost per ticket up ~25%. Worth it given refund dollar exposure. Diminishing returns beyond this without better validation-tool reliability.

hypothesis: We can build an offline replay eval that measures wrongful-refund precision/recall against logged human-resolved tickets, so we can iterate without live A/B risk.
dataset_name: support_tickets_2025 (8,400 tickets with human ground-truth actions)
method: replay agent decisions against logged ground-truth refund/no-refund labels; report precision, recall, dollar-weighted error
conclusion: Replay harness is the cheap iteration signal for all refund work. Dollar-weighted error matters more than raw rate because high-value refunds dominate losses. Use this as the gate for any refund-policy change.

hypothesis: Routing low-confidence refund decisions to a human (instead of acting) will cut dollar-weighted error with an acceptable human-review load.
method: self-reported + logprob-derived confidence; route below-threshold refunds to human queue
conclusion: Threshold 0.8 routes 14% of refunds to humans and cuts dollar-weighted error to 2%. Good operating point. Confidence calibration is rough (self-report over-confident); a calibrated verifier would let us lower the human-review rate.

hypothesis: Full-parameter fine-tuning of Llama-3-8B on our proprietary tabular financial corpus will produce a strong domain model for portfolio-update reasoning.
dataset_name: proprietary_finance_tabular_v1 (~2TB, market+portfolio records serialized to text)
conclusion: Aborted at 40% of epoch 1 after burning ~$24k. Full fine-tune on 2TB is wildly over budget: optimizer states for 8B params + data volume = unsustainable. Mandate going forward: parameter-efficient methods only. This blowup triggered the compute-budget memo.

hypothesis: LoRA fine-tuning of Llama-3-8B reaches comparable task quality to full fine-tuning at a fraction of the compute.
method: LoRA (PEFT) on attention + MLP projections, 1x A100-80GB
conclusion: LoRA hits 0.81 portfolio-update accuracy training <1% of params at ~$110 vs the aborted $24k full run. Clear winner on cost. Quality likely improvable with rank tuning and more data. PEFT is the right family for this corpus.

hypothesis: QLoRA (4-bit NF4 quantized base + LoRA) matches LoRA quality while cutting memory enough to train on a single smaller GPU and scale to more data.
method: QLoRA: 4-bit NF4 base, double quantization, paged AdamW, 1x A100-40GB
conclusion: QLoRA matches/slightly beats LoRA (0.83 vs 0.81) on 5x more data, peak memory 22GB so it fits a 40GB card. Best cost/quality so far for the tabular finance task. Recommended default for scaling to the full corpus.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted tree baseline (XGBoost) on the structured tabular features is competitive with the fine-tuned LLM for the numeric portfolio-update task, at trivial cost.
method: gradient-boosted trees on engineered tabular features (no text serialization)
conclusion: XGBoost gets 0.79 at ~$3 — nearly matches QLoRA on the pure numeric task. The LLM only pulls ahead where free-text rationale / natural-language client instructions are involved. Lesson: use the LLM only for the language-heavy slice; trees handle the tabular core cheaply.

hypothesis: DoRA (weight-decomposed LoRA) beats vanilla LoRA at equal rank on the tabular finance task, justifying the switch.
method: DoRA vs LoRA ablation at matched rank/budget
conclusion: DoRA edges LoRA 0.85 vs 0.83 at equal rank for +18% train time. Small but real gain. For the final full-corpus run, DoRA is worth it if accuracy is the priority; QLoRA if cost is. Did not test DoRA+4bit together — open question.
